items.q: What happens to the total resistance when you add another resistor in parallel?
items.correct: Total resistance decreases — there are more paths for current to flow.
items.misconception: Adding any resistor always increases the total resistance.
items.random_wrong: Adding a parallel resistor doubles the voltage available.
items.why: Students apply the series rule (resistances add) to parallel circuits because they don't visualise multiple paths reducing the overall opposition.

items.q: What is the formula for Young's modulus?
items.correct: Stress / strain — the ratio in the linear (elastic) region of a stress-strain graph.
items.misconception: Force / area — the same as stress itself.
items.random_wrong: Extension / original length — the same as strain.
items.why: Students remember the component words (stress, strain) but mix up that Young's modulus is their ratio, not one of them alone.

items.q: What is the difference between the motor effect and electromagnetic induction?
items.correct: The motor effect produces a force on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field; induction produces an emf when flux linkage changes.
items.misconception: They are the same phenomenon described in different words.
items.random_wrong: The motor effect only works on DC; induction only works on AC.
items.why: Students see both involve coils, magnets and electricity and don't separate 'current causes force' (motor) from 'changing flux causes emf' (induction).
The answer examiners want is: σ4πR²T⁴. “σT⁴” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Star with parallax 0.1 arcsec is at distance:
items.why: It's 10 pc. “0.1 pc” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Star apparent m = 5, absolute M = 0. Distance in pc:
items.why: Exact answer: 100. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
